---
title: "Project Management"
sidebar: arcs01
---

::: {.callout-note}
## By the end of this section, you should be able to...
- Initialize an R environment using `renv` for reproducible package management
- Organize your project directory with a clear folder structure
- Write a README file that documents your project for yourself and others
- Use `fs::dir_tree()` to capture and document your project structure
- Understand the purpose of the metadata cleaning and file naming scripts
:::

---

## Slides

[Open slides fullscreen](02-slides.html){target="_blank"}

<iframe src="02-slides.html" width="100%" height="500px" style="border:none;"></iframe>

## Why Project Management?

Good project management is not just about staying organized — it is a core
part of reproducible research. When you structure your project consistently
from the start, you make it easier for collaborators, reviewers, and your
future self to understand, rerun, and build on your work.

In this workshop series, we embed reproducibility skills directly into
foundational coding instruction. This section introduces two practices you
will use in every project going forward: initializing a reproducible R
environment and writing documentation.

---

## Creating an R Environment

We use `renv` to manage package versions in your project. This ensures that
anyone who clones your repository can restore the exact same R environment
you used — no more "it works on my machine" problems.

1. Navigate to the **Console** tab (left side of the panel)

2. Enter `getwd()` to check that you are in the directory you cloned. If not, navigate there before continuing.

```r
getwd()
```
3. Once you are in the correct directory, enter 
```r
renv::init()
```
::: {.callout-note}
## What `renv::init()` does

- Creates an `renv/` folder
- Creates an `renv.lock` file
- Creates or updates `.gitignore` to exclude the `renv` package cache
- Snapshots any packages already detected in the project 
:::

4. Restart r

::: {.callout-tip}
## In the console
You can do this with the circular arrow icon in the upper right of the console window.
::: 

5. To capture your current environment, enter:

```r
renv::snapshot()
```
This records the package versions currently in use.

::: {.callout-tip}
## In the console

If you get a message that says you are "in a project that hasn't been activated yet", choose option `1: Activate the project and use the project library`
:::

6. Take a look at your project directory in the `Explorer` pane in Positron. In your project directory, you will see new files created by `renv`:

```r
├── .Rprofile
├── renv.lock
└── renv/
    └── .gitignore    # renv's gitignore, scoped only to the renv/ folder
    └── activate.R
    └── settings.json
    └── library/
```
Now when this repository is cloned by a collaborator or reviewer, `renv::restore()` can find the renv files you have created and automatically load the matching package versions.

---

## Write a README file

A README is the entry point to your project. It tells anyone who opens your repository — including your future self — what the project is, what the data is, and how to use it.

1. Confirm that you are in the root directory of your workshop folder:
```r
getwd()
```

2. In the console, enter: 
```r
fs::dir_tree()
```
This calls the dir_tree function from the `fs` package - you should see an output that shows you all your project files and their relationships

3. To write this output to a file, enter this command in the console: 
```r
capture.output(fs::dir_tree(), file = "README.txt")
```

4. Copy the text in the lines below (stop when you get to step 5) and paste at the top of your README.txt file (open in another window):

```r
This readme file was generated on [YYYY-MM-DD] by [NAME]

GENERAL INFORMATION

Title of Dataset:

Author/Principal Investigator Information

Name: ORCID: Institution: Address: Email:

Date of data collection: provide single date, range, or approximate date; suggested format YYYY-MM-DD)

Geographic location of data collection: provide latitude, longitude, or city/region, State, Country

SHARING/ACCESS INFORMATION

DATA & FILE OVERVIEW

File List:

This README template is a simplified version of Cornell's Readme Template for Data: 
https://ecommons.cornell.edu/entities/publication/6effe964-798e-4c21-aac2-4194709ff230 
```

5. Optional: in your README.txt file, write a short description next to these folders: 
- `raw_data (original data goes here)` 
- `processed data (clean data goes here)` 
- `scripts (code to for analyses goes here)` 
- `outputs (analyses outputs go here)`

---

## Scripts for Project Management

The following scripts are used in this workshop series. They are designed to be run in your local Positron session — copy them into your scripts/ folder and run them from there.

::: {.callout-important}
Before Running These Scripts

These scripts load data files from your local raw_data/ folder. Make sure you have completed Exercises 1 and 2 and that your folder structure is in place before running them. 
:::

#### Metadata Cleaning for Analysis

This script creates the metadata file needed for further analysis, using the same dataset contrasted with Excel earlier in this workshop.

```r
# Metadata Cleaning for Analysis
# This script creates the metadata file that we need for further analysis

library(dplyr)
library(stringr)

# Load data
metadata <- read.csv("raw_data/GSE113754_filtered_metadata.csv",
                     header = TRUE,
                     stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

# Create the desired output format
metadata_parsed <- metadata %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Clean up genotype column - convert "Shank3 Mutant" to "Shank3"
    genotype = case_when(
      str_detect(genotype, "WT") ~ "WT",
      str_detect(genotype, "Shank3") ~ "Shank3",
      TRUE ~ as.character(genotype)
    ),

    # Extract condition from characteristics column,
    # format and store in a new column called "Condition"
    Condition = case_when(
      str_detect(characteristics, "homecage control") ~ "_normal",
      str_detect(characteristics, "sleep deprivation") ~ "_sleep_deprived",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    ),

    # Create Geno_Cond column by combining results from the
    # "genotype" and "Condition" column mutations
    Geno_Cond = paste(genotype, str_remove(Condition, "^_"), sep = "_")
  ) %>%
  # Select only the columns you want in the final output
  dplyr::select(Sample = 1, genotype, Condition, Geno_Cond)

# Check the result
head(metadata_parsed)

# Save to CSV
write.csv(metadata_parsed, "Shank3_metadata_parsed.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```

#### Automate File Naming

This script demonstrates how to automatically add a timestamp to any R function and store the result in your outputs/ folder as a new file each time you call it. This ensures you never accidentally overwrite a previous result.

```r
# Automate File Naming
# Automatically timestamps output files and stores them in the outputs/ folder

library(readxl)
library(writexl)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

# Configuration section — update these paths for your project
CONFIG <- list(
  input_file = "raw_data/GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data.xlsx",
  output_folder = "outputs",
  prefix = "LD_ProjectARCS"
)

# Function to generate timestamped filename
generate_filename <- function(prefix, analysis_type = "", extension = ".xlsx") {
  timestamp <- format(Sys.time(), "%Y%m%dT%H%M%S")  # ISO 8601 format
  if (analysis_type != "") {
    filename <- paste0(timestamp, "_", prefix, "_", analysis_type, extension)
  } else {
    filename <- paste0(timestamp, "_", prefix, extension)
  }
  return(filename)
}

# Function to ensure output directory exists
ensure_output_dir <- function(output_folder) {
  if (!dir.exists(output_folder)) {
    dir.create(output_folder, recursive = TRUE)
    cat("Created output directory:", output_folder, "\n")
  }
}

# Function to load Excel data
load_excel_data <- function(file_path, sheet = 1) {
  cat("Loading data from:", file_path, "\n")
  data <- read_excel(file_path, sheet = sheet)
  cat("Data loaded successfully. Dimensions:", dim(data), "\n")
  return(data)
}

# Function to save results
save_results <- function(data, output_folder, prefix, analysis_type = "") {
  ensure_output_dir(output_folder)
  filename <- generate_filename(prefix, analysis_type)
  full_path <- file.path(output_folder, filename)
  write_xlsx(data, full_path)
  cat("Results saved to:", full_path, "\n")
  return(full_path)
}

# Analysis 1: Basic summary statistics
analysis_summary_stats <- function(df, output_folder, prefix) {
  cat("\n=== Running Summary Statistics Analysis ===\n")
  summary_data <- df %>%
    summarise_if(is.numeric, list(
      mean = ~mean(., na.rm = TRUE),
      median = ~median(., na.rm = TRUE),
      sd = ~sd(., na.rm = TRUE),
      min = ~min(., na.rm = TRUE),
      max = ~max(., na.rm = TRUE)
    ))
  save_path <- save_results(summary_data, output_folder, prefix, "summary_stats")
  return(summary_data)
}

# Analysis 2: Filtered data analysis
analysis_filtered_data <- function(df, output_folder, prefix,
                                   filter_condition = NULL) {
  cat("\n=== Running Filtered Data Analysis ===\n")
  if (!is.null(filter_condition)) {
    processed_data <- df %>%
      filter(eval(parse(text = filter_condition))) %>%
      arrange(desc(if(is.numeric(.[,1])) .[,1] else row_number()))
  } else {
    processed_data <- df %>%
      arrange(if(is.numeric(.[,1])) desc(.[,1]) else .[,1])
  }
  save_path <- save_results(processed_data, output_folder, prefix,
                            "filtered_data")
  return(processed_data)
}

# Analysis 3: Grouped analysis
analysis_grouped <- function(df, output_folder, prefix,
                             group_by_col = NULL) {
  cat("\n=== Running Grouped Analysis ===\n")
  if (!is.null(group_by_col) && group_by_col %in% names(df)) {
    grouped_data <- df %>%
      group_by(across(all_of(group_by_col))) %>%
      summarise_if(is.numeric, list(
        count = ~n(),
        mean = ~mean(., na.rm = TRUE),
        sum = ~sum(., na.rm = TRUE)
      ), .groups = 'drop')
  } else {
    first_char_col <- names(select_if(df, is.character))[1]
    if (!is.null(first_char_col)) {
      grouped_data <- df %>%
        count(across(all_of(first_char_col)), name = "frequency") %>%
        arrange(desc(frequency))
    } else {
      grouped_data <- data.frame(note = "No grouping column available")
    }
  }
  save_path <- save_results(grouped_data, output_folder, prefix,
                            "grouped_analysis")
  return(grouped_data)
}

# Main execution function
run_analysis <- function() {
  cat("=== Starting Analysis Pipeline ===\n")
  cat("Timestamp:", format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), "\n\n")

  original_data <- load_excel_data(CONFIG$input_file)
  working_data <- original_data

  results <- list()
  results$summary <- analysis_summary_stats(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)
  results$filtered <- analysis_filtered_data(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)
  results$grouped <- analysis_grouped(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)

  cat("\n=== Analysis Pipeline Complete ===\n")
  cat("All results saved to:", CONFIG$output_folder, "\n")
  return(results)
}

# Helper function to run individual analyses
run_single_analysis <- function(analysis_type, ...) {
  original_data <- load_excel_data(CONFIG$input_file)
  working_data <- original_data
  switch(analysis_type,
    "summary"  = analysis_summary_stats(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix),
    "filtered" = analysis_filtered_data(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix, ...),
    "grouped"  = analysis_grouped(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix, ...),
    stop("Unknown analysis type. Use 'summary', 'filtered', or 'grouped'")
  )
}

# To use this script:
# 1. Update the CONFIG section above with your file paths
# 2. Run all analyses:
results <- run_analysis()

# Or run individual analyses:
summary_result <- run_single_analysis("summary")
# filtered_result <- run_single_analysis("filtered",
#                      filter_condition = "column_name > 50")
grouped_result <- run_single_analysis("grouped",
                    group_by_col = "category")
```
::: {.callout-note}
Can You...?

    ✓ Initialize an R environment with renv::init()?
    ✓ Explain what renv.lock is and why it matters for reproducibility?
    ✓ Use fs::dir_tree() to inspect your project structure?
    ✓ Write and populate a README file for a project?
    ✓ Explain what the metadata cleaning script does at a high level?
    ✓ Explain why timestamped file naming helps prevent data loss? 
:::
